chunks.text: Good morning. Morning, Minister. We've set aside 15 minutes to talk about the Agricultural Compounds and Veterinary Medicines Amendment Bill, so hook into it. Okay. Probably don't need to talk about the problem statement. I think we all got that from the first reading and all the evidence you've heard up to now.

In terms of the bill itself, I think what I might just cover off quickly is what we're trying to solve is to increase that speed, make sure these products are getting to market in a clear and an improved timeline. We don't want to be spending years and years. As you heard from some of the submitters on the HASNO, New Zealand's a very small market in the world.

If it seems like it's too hard a job to do here in New Zealand, those companies don't come here. Our farmers, growers, don't get the opportunity to use the latest technology, the newest drugs, chemicals, whatever. And so it's important that we're a market that people want to be involved with, and so we do need to look at how we can make the process a hell of a lot more efficient. I think the key thing with the bill is relying on some of those trusted overseas jurisdictions.

We do have an MOU with Australia, so there's elements in this bill that's going to make it a lot more user-friendly in terms of ensuring that we take advantage of the work that's already been done in Australia, to actually get approvals much faster here in New Zealand.

I think you may have guessed from my question in the previous thing around the-- and I assume this is going to be a very similar question that you're going to have placed in front of you in terms of these timelines. Are they best in primary legislation? Are they best in regulation?

I've got a feeling that my personal belief and where we've landed at has been that if we've got them in regulation, we can target particularly those new, novel, ingredients, the stuff that our farmers and growers need, so we can set precise timelines for that to be delivered.

At the moment, we do have it in primary legislation, and we're not really achieving it anywhere near fast enough, and it's often we're able to do the simple stuff really quickly, but it's that harder stuff that we need to be a bit more focused on. So my feeling is, certainly I'd be more inclined to make sure we stick with where it is in regulation.

And there's some other matters in there where the use of overseas information and stuff, there's-- I can't remember the wording off by hand, but it's sort of at the direction of the director general. And so there's questions there, I think, and the industry's rightly pointing out that they are concerned with that certainty that things will be done.

And so, I would really encourage the committee to have a good look at how can we not only speed up this process, ensure that all the safeguards are in place, but at the same time, where we position these things, be it primary regulation or discretion.

There's that trade-off between flexibility and certainty for industry, and so certainly be interested in any feedback when you're through this process, any feedback the select committee has on whether you think changes could or should be made, to enable, trying to find that ideal balance point between flexibility and certainty.

Would it be fair to say that we're losing capability and scientists offshore because there's a reticence to invest in R&D by some of the companies that once did invest here in New Zealand? Yeah. You've seen this. And I can't say whether it's solely attributable to the time lag that it's taking to get stuff approved by through HASNO and ACVM, but it is happening. And so we've got to make sure that we provide a regulatory environment that encourages companies to come here to think of- New Zealand

as the innovative agricultural place where they want to be investing in, and that they do this research and stuff here in this country. We've certainly got the innovative farmers that'll be taking advantage of it, so I think that's an important thing. Steve, you had a question. No?

Any other questions? No. Don't want to know whether Halter's impacted by Well, go on, tell us. No. No. It was funny. That came up- Sorry. Inside joke. Yeah. Ah. Very good. All right. Thanks very much. No worries. And- Like I say, I'm quite interested and, as you go through this process, any sort of feedback you've got, I'm more than interested. If you think there's value changes that could be made to help, not only strengthen the system, speed it up, and give certainty industry, more than interested in hearing them.

Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you. I think the only, the biggest fear of that is with 10 million dollars. Yeah. Well, that hopefully the vets will talk about. Right. So, going to move on to the hearing of evidence. First up is the New Zealand Vegetable Council, and James and Jay are back.

Yeah. Thank you. Sorry, James had to go back to the farm. Oh. So it's just me. But- Somebody's got to. There's Jay. Someone's got to work... So just James. Yeah. Yeah. So, I think Jay did a fantastic job this morning to really articulate the concerns from the vegetable growers' perspectives. Yeah. And so this, I guess, he also dovetailed into the ACVM side a bit, so we'll try and just articulate that position now.

So, chair, thank you very much for your time today. My name is James Koufos from the New Zealand Vegetable Council. We represent New Zealand's vegetable growers, processors, and exporters across New Zealand, which right now are going through a fairly difficult time, but we're always encouraged by looking forward. ACVM, like the HSNO Act, are critically important for the vegetable sector.

So the key difference, I guess, from the vegetable side of things, compared to perhaps our larger colleagues around the primary sector, is just the size of the crops that we're growing and the label extension. So, we have the 0.1 default MRL in New Zealand, which is superb. I don't think we could operate without that.

And so we need to make sure we maintain that position around the maximum residue limits. What we're kind of seeking, I guess, is the ease to be able to register products for these crops, for these niche crops. Because, the default MRL of 0.1 is a great fallback position, but if the product is actually registered at a different rate, a different residue limit, we might be scoring an own goal here in New Zealand where we say, "Actually, now you've got to have a withholding period of 30 days or 40 days." And so for some of these crops, like spinach,

that's just not feasible with how quick the crop grows. No. So that's where we're getting stuck is saying, "Right, the default is superb, and we don't want to touch that or look at removing that." But the headache we've got is getting those label registrations for these niche crops, and particularly whether, niche like celery or spinach, et cetera, where there's just no financial incentive for multinationals to come to New Zealand right now. So, very, very complicated and tough for our sector, but fundamental as well.

We touched on resistance management earlier on. So, without having the ability to rotate these different products and, yeah, there's a lot of discussion around biopesticides, but also other novel products coming through that we need to make sure we have a pathway for future products we don't have a label for yet, or we don't know about yet. So we're talking mostly about synthetic chemistry, and we talk about biopesticides, but there are other novel controls that we need to also keep in mind, keep open to in the future as well. So just making sure we capture that for

the legislation. And I guess the other one that we want to highlight is around biosecurity incursions. So when we do have incursions, yeah, there's rapid pathways and the likes, but we think it's more attractive to try and maintain these products for New Zealand, so we've got them ready to go rather than trying to register them, and get approval later on. Because that pathway can take six-plus months and by that stage, whatever we're trying to control is out of control, out of our hands. So we just need to be mindful

as well about how do we manage those kind of incursions and make sure we're prepared as best as we can. Probably the final point that we want to make is around the legislation is probably a third of the solution. The other two thirds are around resourcing and staffing for the departments, for EPA and MPI, and also making sure that they've got the right culture, to actually implement the legislation. We had the HSNO Amendment Act four years ago.

We're going back over through the HSNO Act now, but the culture, which we're seeing really positive changes right now to actually say we need to be more permissible, find ways to work through these solutions as well. We think that's equally as important as the legislation, and we're really encouraged by both EPA and MPI in terms of the changes in staff culture to actually say, "We've got to find solutions here." So, MPI colleagues in particular are looking at the Codex standards, so falling back to those instead of

having to go to defaults, as well. That's a really sensible approach that the officials are taking, and so it's something that may not be captured in the legislation, but we should acknowledge the good work that officials actually are taking to get there. The resourcing, the backlog pipeline, the legislation may help us, but we're still going to have a big backlog to actually clear. And so we're also supportive of trying to address that backlog however possible, if it's temporary resourcing, just so we get rid of that five-year plus backlog and we get back

to where we should be and move forward. Yeah. So those are the main points that we wish to Just to reiterate, we think New Zealand's a fantastic place to be growing vegetables. We want to continue, and this legislation will help us a lot. Thank you. Could you give us an example of something that is covered by the ACVM Act and not the HSNO Act that you'd be, as Vegetable Oil New Zealand- Yep... interested in having a pathway speed up for?

Yeah, sure. So, it's probably the label extension. So the one we touched on earlier on, Movento, in Australia and is registered in New Zealand. So it can be used in New Zealand already for those crops it's registered for, and it can be used off-label. The headache we have is we want to promote the use of some of these products, but currently we can't do that. So it actually is a really good IPM product to be able to say it doesn't harm the beneficial insects in the field and is targeted towards the thrips.

We can't promote that as industry bodies right now to say, "You should be using this product because it's softer," because it's not registered on the field crops that we represent. So that would be an example where we're a little bit stuck. We kind of- Mm... hope that our members come to the appropriate position. Yeah. Steve. Steve, so sup on that. So for comprehension of the layperson, you're allowed to use this stuff.

Yep. It's in New Zealand right now being used. You're not allowed to promote it. Yeah, correct. So even if it's a softer product- Yeah... we can't be promoting something that's not registered for our crops, I guess. Yeah. Yeah. And so why aren't you allowed to promote it? It's probably one for the officials, but yeah, we just try to follow the rules, I guess, on it but- Because there's a regulatory barrier to you promoting.

Yeah. Yeah. So, yeah. Because it hasn't been through the New Zealand system for human health implications. Yeah. But how has it not been through the system if you're using it? Because we're consuming it right now, if there's any- Yeah... residues or whatever. Yeah, so it's registered for potatoes, but not for spinach or something. Right. Yeah. So we can promote it for potato use, but we can't promote it for spinach use, for example. Yep.

Why is that? Yep. Ugh. My understanding is just in the regulations then. But yeah. Is it because there's different residues or different... Because you're using it on the foliage of the potato, not on the- Yeah. But it comes back to the default MRL still, back to the 0.1. So yeah, the- ... human risk doesn't change. Yeah.

But is it because on the actual bit of the potato that you eat, it hasn't been applied because that's in the- It's systemic, is it? It's not really, like, it doesn't go into the use practices or- Right... anything. It's just, yeah, we default back to the residue limit. Yep. Mm. Right. And so there would be more residue if you used it on spinach because it's going straight on the foliage that you eat.

It's not that there's more residue or anything. It's still just that there's no registration for it, so we default back to that 0.1. Yep. So it's not about the use or crop or, yeah. Mm. Okay. Thank you. Is this what Jay was talking about before with the broccoli and not being able to use it on the spinach? The drug for the... I can't remember.

Was it something rust or something that- Yeah. Yeah. That's right. Yeah. But we've also got to be careful with holding periods. So it's not the actual effects that you're measuring, it's just that one's registered so you can do the actual effects and the other one's not registered, so you have to log it at the highest possible.

Yeah. Yeah. So it's- So it's not actually, for the one that's not registered, you're not actually It seems like a- Yeah... silly thing. Jay's one was that you might have it registered, and it might be at five instead of 0.1, so you've got a greater tolerance for the product.

So you can use it right up until like five days before harvest or something because it's a more benign product or something. Oh, okay. So his point was, if you're at five parts per million, for the broccoli, you can use it till five days in advance. Whereas if you're off-label and you've got it dropped down to 0.1 now, then you'd need to cut off at like 30 days in advance just because there's no label or guidance on it. Yep.

Yeah. Yep. But it doesn't actually reflect the safety of the product necessarily. No, because it's registered already- Yeah... in like Australia or something. Yeah. So yeah, we just- Mm... get a little bit stuck here. So it's to do with off-label use. Yeah. So- For them, yeah. And on. And the limits. Mm.

Yeah, because the default is back to that 0.1. So if there's a higher threshold for different products, for different crops, you can go to that threshold. Would there be- So it's like a default if it's not registered. Yeah, I understand. Yeah. Would there be- So it's not actually reflecting the effects. Yeah. Would there be export considerations around that- Um... do you think?

Yeah. So there are export headaches as well. So if we export to Europe or UK and to retailers, they ask us for, "What's your list of registered products in New Zealand?" And we kind of had to say, "Oh, geez, here's the list. But we're allowed to use these other ones as well, but you know." And that's a complicated conversation to have.

Yeah. I can believe it. To kind of say, "Oh, these are the ones." And they kind of say, "Oh, you're using these ones as well." And it's like, "Yeah, like there's a New Zealand pathway here." So, that's a headache. We would prefer to be using registered products- Yeah... clearly. But, we also don't want to get rid of that off-label pathway, because that would really, really damage the efficacy. Yeah.

Steve- Just for clarity though, the on-label rules are designed around the- Principles of what safe ways to use those chemicals, right? So you're not meant to be using stuff off-label, and once you start using stuff off-label, you're going outside the parameters that stuff's been approved as the safe use of that product.

I've been not really- No. Hence using- No. It's more like stringent limit that says, "Okay, it's not registered at five." Yeah. You can use the more stringent limit of 0.1 if it's not registered. Maybe talk about- Kind of safeguard. Yeah... is there a reason it's not registered? It's just, it's still cost to register.

Yeah. So that's the reason. And so they're cheaper. I think that's what Jay alluded to, too. Yeah. That the product, it's not the cost of registration precludes it being worth it, doesn't it? Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Any further questions? No. Thanks very much, James. It's okay.

It's complex stuff. Yeah. It's complex stuff. Yeah, it's really detailed, complex, and yeah, we really appreciate the effort to actually dive into it because it's not easy. But we've got to find a way through for New Zealand here. Yeah. What percentage of our fresh vegetables do we export, of the weight, like what you grow, to your significant- Prior to two months ago, would've been more. Now we're probably at 80% domestic for fresh- I would've even thought that would be higher.

Well, we export onions- Oh, okay... about $150 million worth of onions. We export processed vegetables and frozen fries to Australia. Yeah. Yeah. So- But fresh is just onions, basically? Capsicums as well to some markets, and we fly some stuff into Hong Kong, Singapore, Pacific Islands.

We do a lot of trade because- Mm... they can't grow vegetables easily there, so we do a lot of Pacific Island trade for veggies, yeah. Mm. Cool. Thank you very much. Thanks, all. Thanks, James. Thank you, mate. Thank you. We'll move on to Seed and Grain New Zealand. Mm. And we've got Sarah and Cressida back online.

Hello. Hi. How are you? Thanks for having us again. Yes, so I'm Sarah Clark, the Chief Executive of Seed and Grain New Zealand, and I'm joined by Cressida Patrick, our Biosecurity and Technical Manager. So we represent seed and grain companies and also ag chem companies who are supplying chemicals to the seed and grain industry.

Cressida and I are going to do a tag-team submission again. I'm conscious that you've heard our context earlier this morning- Mm-hmm... when we addressed the HASNO bill. But for the record on ACVM submission, I am going to repeat much of our context to set the scene. A key point is that our industry relies on chemicals to produce seeds and grains important to New Zealand's economy. Crop failures are our future if we can't access appropriate chemicals.

So our members are producing grains, including wheat, barley, and oats, and these are used for human consumption and also our animal feed. And our members are producing the seeds for sowing for the domestic, excuse me, and the international markets. It includes grass seed, clover seed, forage, oil seeds, and vegetable seeds. Our industry does have a reputation in the global seed trade for providing very high-quality seeds, and we want to keep it that way, because we're producing 40 to 50% of the world's carrot seed and

60% of the world's radish and clover seed. Why are chemicals important to our industry? Well, the seeds for sowing are treated with a light coating of agrichemical before they're sown, and it really does make a big difference to the health of the crop. It gives the crop a really healthy start because it's protecting the seed and the young shoots and roots in the soil from pests and diseases. And then, of course, we're using chemicals during the growth of the crop, for controlling weeds and pests and diseases, all the way through to

seed set. So yeah, without access to the right chemicals at the right time, farmers and seed companies can't produce that high-quality, high-purity seed and grain that is demanded. There's a world global demand for our seed because of our reputation for doing such a good job.

And hence, because chemicals are so important, the proposed changes to the ACVM Act are something that we want to comment on. There are some aspects that we think can be further improved. I spoke earlier in the HASNO bill hearing about the impact of the regulatory settings on our industry, and those comments are equally relatable to the ACVM Bill, so I will emphasize them again.

The regulations have a direct impact on the sustainability and productiveness of arable farms and seed companies. And a key reason why that matters is the codependency between the arable sector and the other primary sectors. So any positive or negative impact on arable farms does flow through all the way to our dairy farms and sheep farms, whose export products are underpinning wealth generation in New Zealand.

And secondly, the regulations will impact our ability to supply seeds back to the international market. And the key issue there is that the EU in particular is prohibiting agrochemicals faster than we can approve chemicals that they do allow. So, we will be unable to use new or novel chemistries unless our approval process is quick pace. I am going to hand across to Chris. She's got three points, technical points that she would like to address. Thank you.

Sarah. So as with the Hasno Amendment Bill, we're supporting modernizing the ACVM framework. We believe this will provide a more proportionate risk-based regulatory system, and we support increasing alignment with the trusted international regulators. These are reforms that our sector's been advocating for for many years.

The submission has three key points. The first one is that we support the proposal to allow the Director-General to exempt lower-risk agricultural compounds from registration requirements. We consider exemptions are an important part of modern risk-based regulatory frameworks and lower-risk products, such as biostimulants, which are often cited as a crop, a probiotic rather than a chemistry, is considered a lower-risk compound and shouldn't face the same regulatory burden as a higher-risk compound.

We think moving these exemptions from Cabinet to the Director-General will make the process more efficient and responsive, and we consider this as a very positive move. However, we would like to urge that transparent criteria is used, and that clear evidence requirements and clear explanations when an application is declined, is provided by the Director-General to the appropriate people. We believe this will help ensure the consistency, fairness, transparency, and confidence in the decision-making process that you will have heard a lot of the applicants are reliant on.

Secondly, we believe the bill should go further in recognizing assessments undertaken by the trusted overseas regulators. We would like to acknowledge the work that the New Zealand Food Safety has done in the Trans-Tasman pilot for manufacturers and agrochemicals to support registration in both countries, and we would support more initiatives like this.

Where a product has already undergone a rigorous scientific assessment by a recognized regulator such as Australia, Canada, EU, US, these assessments should form the primary basis for the decision-making in New Zealand, unless there's a clearly identified New Zealand-specific consideration. As currently drafted, we consider that the bill gives significant discretion to the Director-General, but doesn't necessarily provide that feedback loop to the applicant that provides the certainty that they need.

The overseas assessments may be considered, but there's no clear expectation that they will be relied upon. We recommend stronger requirements for transparency, including clear criteria for recognition decision-making, and again, we'd like to emphasize that explanation should be provided back to the applicant where an overseas assessment is not relied upon.

Thirdly, we consider that there is the missed opportunity to focus on that, establishing the dedicated pathway for biopesticides and as James alluded to, as well as other newer novel chemistry, newer techniques that we may not necessarily be aware of.

The New Zealand framework treats many biopesticides in a similar manner to conventional synthetic pesticides, despite their generally lower risk profiles. We believe that this creates higher compliance cost, regulatory uncertainty, and obviously the lengthy approval time frames.

As alluded to in our earlier presentation, the biopesticides and other newer techniques are becoming increasingly important offshore as the older synthetic chemistries are being phased out. We believe that ensuring a framework that supports these newer technologies can help manage resistance, reduce reliance on the older chemistry, and support access to the domestic markets that we need to continue accessing.

I'll just give two quick examples. So domestically, we've had the loss of chlorpyrifos, and we've got the upcoming loss of diazinon, two of the main controls for grass grub, a hugely important New Zealand endemic pest species that's not present anywhere else, that has a huge impact on the arable sector.

We are going to become increasingly reliant on these biopesticides such as BioShield and Verterblast to help control New Zealand grass grubs. Unfortunately, these biopesticides take a number of years to actually establish and grow in the actual fields themselves and also limit the synthetic chemistries that can be used when they have been applied.

So there's some considerations that our farmers will need to actually consider in an upcoming on-the-ground example that's going to happen. Globally, the EU again is one of our main markets, and as they phase out the synthetic chemistry, we will not be able to supply treated seed that has the older chemistries that are available to us.

Other jurisdictions such as Australia, Canada, US, and EU have developed more tailored and proportionate pathways for biopesticides and some of those other lower-risk compounds, and we recommend the bill considers including an enabling provision that allows these dedicated pathways in New Zealand.

In closing, Seed and Grain support the overall direction of the ACVM Amendment Bill, and we welcome your commitment to working on the framework. We consider this as a long overdue opportunity, and we are excited to see genuine reform, but we do consider further work is needed.

We encourage the committee to strengthen the bill by providing greater certainty to the applicants around the recognition of trusted international assessment, and also we really encourage that transparency for applicants to ensure those business cases remain justified and eligible within New Zealand.

New Zealand growers and seed companies need a regulatory system that can keep pace with innovation without jeopardising New Zealand's human health or the environment. By getting these settings right, we believe we will support a more productive, sustainable, and internationally competitive future for our sector and for the wider agricultural economy.

Thanks again for your opportunity to speak to our submission, and we'd welcome any questions. Thank you. We've got Steve. Yeah, I've got a few questions. Go for it. Which agrochemical companies do you represent? So we have several members. I actually am not entirely sure if I can say that.

My apologies. That's not something I've had to consider. We have- You can tell us... yeah, we've got- It's in the public domain. It is on our webpage, so yeah. Okay. Yeah. I fear I might miss some off if I start-... saying. So, BASF, Bayer- Syngenta... Syngenta. There are others, and I apologize if I can't remember.

That's all good. That's good. Apologize to those. Yeah. I mean, those are the big, bad ones, so that's good to know. The, yeah. I would- The, so I got another question on that. I mean, that's useful to understand. Are you still using paraquat with your clover production? Hmm. That is a question I don't have an answer to. We can find out, though, and get that back to you.

And- Do you think it's-... is that a problem, is that gonna be a problem in the EU? I mean, paraquat's one of the chemicals that's been banned in dozens of countries now. Still legal to use here and still legal to use in Australia, but is that an example of an old synthetic chemical that's highly toxic that we're gonna have to move away from, and you need some other options?

Other options are always good. I mean, we advocate for safer chemicals. What we're saying is we need chemicals that protect the crops, and we absolutely want safer chemicals. In terms of the ability for us to export seeds to the EU, the issue is more about the chemicals that are used to coat the seed, and I don't think paraquat is one of those.

No, it's not. I mean, I know paraquat is used in clover production, so it's useful- It's a desiccant. Yeah. So you're not using it in New Zealand. Is that the message? Not a seed treatment in Gordium. And then, in terms of, I don't know, I'll leave it at there because other people have some questions, but that's useful. Thank you.

Susan. Look, we know that arable farmers have been doing it tough when everyone else seems to be doing remarkably well at the moment, and I was just wondering, are you able to say that with the changes to this legislation that it will actually, and we've heard it from other submitters, increase productivity, reduce costs, and also have the ability to reduce the environmental impact of- With the combined changes to HSNO and ACVM, with the aim of making the approvals of chemicals more efficient, yes.

We need more of, a wider variety of chemicals, and yeah, anything that is going to do that and provide more choice for farmers and reduce the compliance costs is going to be a good outcome. The longer an application process is for the chemical companies, that's a cost to them, and then they have to pass that on to their customers. So, efficient systems are cheaper, and that flows through to end users.
Chris, did you want to add anything to that?

Doesn't seem that she does. No, sorry. I've got a follow-up. Look, no- Yeah, sure... we're out of time, so. Oh, shoot. Okay. Thank you very much, Sarah and Chris, for- Thank you. Thanks for your time... taking the time and being patient with us this morning. Cheers. Right. We're gonna move on to Dairy NZ.

Just remind you, Steve, that the paraquat is in HSNO, won't be under ACVM. Hmm. Yeah. That's right. Yeah. Welcome again. Thank you. And thank you again for giving me the opportunity to talk today. As with others, much of what is in our submission for ACVM is very similar to the HSNO submission that we put in.

But I'm Carol Boneo, Principal Advisor in the biosecurity space for Dairy NZ, and I talk on behalf of Dairy NZ, the industry good organization that represents 11,000 dairy farmers. So why does this matter to our farmers? At the core, this bill matters because it determines how quickly and how reliably farmers have access to the job they need to do. Farmers are at the forefront of a lot of New Zealand's biggest challenges, climate change, emerging biosecurity risks, and rising expectations around animal health and welfare.

And right now, you've heard that there is a regulatory system that could slow down the access to new and improved products, whether that's emissions mitigation tools, animal health treatments, or biosecurity risk management tools. For farmers, delays in access don't just create frustration.

They can have a direct impact on farm resilience, animal welfare outcomes, and our ability to respond to biosecurity risks quickly and efficiently. So we strongly support the intent of the bill to improve efficiency, transparency, and certainty for the approvals process. The main points that we had in our submission were really around improving access through proportionate regulation and the current regulatory pathways affect farmers in two main ways. One, it limits the ability to research and test new greenhouse gas mitigation

solutions, and that's a significant issue for the dairy industry, and it also delays access for tools that are needed to manage emerging pests and diseases. And that also is a huge concern for us. And we are investing a lot in biosecurity readiness to ensure that we are as prepared as possible, and part of that preparedness is really around the tools that we have available to manage those risks.

We support the changes to introduce a more proportionate approvals pathway with greater use of exemptions for low-risk products and more flexible processes for product changes and applications, which are a very complex, complicated area. These are practical improvements that should reduce unnecessary barriers while maintaining proportionate safeguards, and that's critical to our dairy farmers in terms of public health and also in terms of market access, which goes on to the next topic around recognizing overseas regular assessments. We also strongly

support the proposal that requires the director general to take into account decisions from overseas regulators that are recognized. In practical terms, this means we should avoid duplication where products have already been robustly assessed, sometimes in our key markets that we're sending product to, streamlining New Zealand's approval processes and improving access to products that have already been proven in comparable agricultural systems.

For farmers and veterinarians, this is critical, and timely access to efficient tools will strongly influence biosecurity preparedness, animal welfare outcomes, and the ability to respond to new and emerging biosecurity risks, which are many and varied at this particular time.

This is a common-sense change that should improve efficiency without compromising safety or our market access. In terms of timely and transparent decision-making, we welcome the bill's intent for reducing time frames and cost to applicants. Currently, high costs, uncertainty, long delays create real barriers, as you've already heard, particularly for a small market like New Zealand.

This can discourage international suppliers, meaning farmers may have access to fewer or less effective products than their overseas counterparts, and you will have also already heard some of those examples. We also support improvements to the data assessor framework, which should enhance consistency and quality of those assessments, give greater use of external expertise, especially for new and emerging areas, and also support more timely decision-making.

However, we note that the bill has not yet established a clear emergency use pathway, a fast-track process or targeted mechanisms that can show transparently how we can actually recognize and manage niche or urgent products. And that certainly is a critical area for us.

This means that urgent biosecurity or animal welfare needs may still face delays. We encourage the government to continue strengthening the wider regulatory framework to address these gaps. And we also would finally like to emphasize that we are looking for ensuring sound regulatory outcomes in practice.

We would emphasize that much of the real impact of this bill will depend on how it's implemented. The bill provides an important enabling framework, but the devil is in the detail that sits in the regulations that underpin it and how those regulations are operationalized.

Getting those settings right will be critical in delivering faster access to low-risk products, as you've also heard, proportionate decision-making, and continued trust from our international trading partners, and that's a critical issue for the dairy industry. Dairy NZ welcomes the opportunity to engage further as those regulations are developed.

Regulatory efforts should be focused on changes that present genuine risk to product quality, safety, efficacy, trade, or the environment. To improve efficiency of the ACVM regulatory process, we propose that decisions should be based on clearly identified risks, not preferences for particular technical approaches.

Assessors can become prescriptive on technical approaches without clearly articulating the underlying risk or the failure mode being controlled. This can result in repeated rounds of requests, inconsistent advice, and data requirements that are not demonstrably necessary to manage risk.

Such failures have been identified in chemistry manufacturing controls disputes, where process controls are treated as insufficient risk mitigation without a clear statement of the unmitigated risk. Furthermore, when additional conditions are imposed late in the process without being raised during request for information stages, they consequently reduce procedural fairness and increase cost.

By grounding every data request and decision in clearly identified risk rather than technical preferences, we create a proportionate and transparent model that will be backed by a written justification for rejected controls, thus preventing unnecessary rework, and subsequently fosters a more consistent and efficient regulatory environment.

The bill's move towards approved specifications, while positive, presents the caveat that such specifications or associated conditions may become overly prescriptive and effectively lock manufacturing into single processes, even where different approaches can produce equivalent outcomes.
